subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>!</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mrow></mfrac><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>5</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>9</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mo>adj</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>adj</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math>  is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>!</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mrow></mfrac><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>5</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>7</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>9</mn><mo>!</mo></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mo>adj</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>adj</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math>  is equal to
solution:
Given,

A=15!6!7!5!6!7!6!7!8!7!8!9!

⇒A=15!6!7!5!6!7!6!7!8!7!8!9!

⇒A=15!6!7!5!×6!×7!164217561872

⇒|A|=2

Now using the property of adjoint of matrix we get,

adjadjA=A(n-1)2 & kA=knA, where n is order of square matrix,

Now using the above two formula we get,

adjadj2A=2A4=212A4

⇒adjadj2A=212·24

⇒adjadj2A=216

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let A be a $2 \times 2$ matrix
Statement-1 $: \operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} A)=A$
Statement-2 : $|\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A}|=|\mathrm{A}|$
question:
Let A be a $2 \times 2$ matrix
Statement-1 $: \operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} A)=A$
Statement-2 : $|\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A}|=|\mathrm{A}|$
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is a correct explanation for Statement-1
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not a correct explanation for Statement-1
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true

subject: Mathematics
q: The probability that a randomly chosen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>2</mn></math> matrix with all the entries from the set of first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> primes, is singular, is equal to
question: The probability that a randomly chosen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>2</mn></math> matrix with all the entries from the set of first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> primes, is singular, is equal to
solution:
Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{b} \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{d}\end{array}\right]$ where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}$ are prime.
Total number of matrices A formed $=10^4$
Let $\mathrm{S}$ be a matrix from set of matrices $\mathrm{A}$ such that $\mathrm{S}$ is singular.
For singular matrix $|\mathrm{S}|=\mathrm{ad}-\mathrm{bc}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{ad}=\mathrm{bc}$
Number of singular matrices $=$ All entries are same $+$ only two prime number are used in matrix
then number of matrices with two prime numbers $=(10 \times 9) \times 2 !=180$
then number of matrices with one prime number $=10 \times 1=10$
So required probability $=\frac{10 \times 9 \times 2 !+10}{10^4}=\frac{19}{1000}=0.019$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $I$ be the identity matrix of order $3 \times 3$ and for the matrix $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\lambda & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & -1 & 2\end{array}\right],|\mathrm{A}|=-1$. Let B be the inverse of the matrix $\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A} \operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A}^2\right)\right)$. Then $|(\lambda B+1)|$ is equal to _____
question: Let $I$ be the identity matrix of order $3 \times 3$ and for the matrix $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\lambda & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & -1 & 2\end{array}\right],|\mathrm{A}|=-1$. Let B be the inverse of the matrix $\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A} \operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A}^2\right)\right)$. Then $|(\lambda B+1)|$ is equal to _____
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& |A|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\lambda & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & -1 & 2
\end{array}\right|=-1 \\ & \lambda(16)-2(-34)+3(-39)=-1 \\ & 16 \lambda=48 \Rightarrow \lambda=3 \\ & \mathrm{~B}^{-1}=\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A} \cdot \operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{~A}^2\right)\right) \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{A} \cdot \operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{~A}^2\right) \\ & A C=A^2 \operatorname{adj}\left(A^2\right)=|A|^2 \cdot I=I \Rightarrow C=A^{-1} \\ & \text { Now } B^{-1}=\operatorname{adj}\left(A^{-1}\right)=B=\operatorname{adj}(A) \\ & \text { Now } \lambda \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{I} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{I} \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{P}=3 \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{I} \\ & \text { P }=3 \operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{~A})+\mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{AP}=3 \operatorname{Aadj}(\mathrm{~A})+\mathrm{A} \\ & \mathrm{AP}=3|\mathrm{~A}| \cdot \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A} \\ & \mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{A}-3 \mathrm{I} \\ & |\mathrm{AP}|=|\mathrm{A}-3 \mathrm{I}| \\ & |\mathrm{A}| \cdot|\mathrm{P}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 2 & 6 \\ 7 & -1 & -1
\end{array}\right|=38 \\ & |\mathrm{P}|=-38
\end{aligned}$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced></math> , then which one of the following statements is not correct?
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced></math> , then which one of the following statements is not correct?
solution:
A =0-110

A2= 0-1100-110= -100-1

A3=-100-10-110=01-10

A4=01-100-110=1001

Option 1  A3+I= A A3-I

  01 -10  -1-1  1-1= 1-1 11

1-1 11 = 1-1 11 .....Correct

Option 2 A4-I=A2+I

0000= 0000 ....Correct

Option 3 0000= 0-110× -200-2 = 02-20 .....Incorrect

Option 4

A3-I=A(A-I)

-11-1-1= 0-110 × -1-11-1 = -11-1-1

 ......Correct.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math> matrix such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>12</mn><mn>4</mn></msup></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math> matrix such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>12</mn><mn>4</mn></msup></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>a</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>j</mi><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given equation is

adjadjadj.A=124

⇒A(n-1)3=124

∵ n=3

⇒A(3-1)3=124

⇒A8=124

⇒A2=12

⇒A=23

Now, A-1adj A=A-1.adj A=1A×A3-1

=A=23

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A$ be a $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix such that $A\left[\begin{array}{l}1 \\ 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 7\end{array}\right]$ and the determinant of $A$ be 1 . If $A^{-1}=\alpha A+\beta I$, where $I$ is an identity matrix of order $2 \times 2$, then $\alpha+\beta$ equals _______
question: Let $A$ be a $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix such that $A\left[\begin{array}{l}1 \\ 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 7\end{array}\right]$ and the determinant of $A$ be 1 . If $A^{-1}=\alpha A+\beta I$, where $I$ is an identity matrix of order $2 \times 2$, then $\alpha+\beta$ equals _______
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}a & b \\ b & d\end{array}\right] \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ll}a & b \\ b & d\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}1 \\ 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 7\end{array}\right], a d-b^2=1} \\ & a+b=3, b+d=7,(3-b)(7-b)-b^2=1 \\ & 21-10 b=1 \rightarrow b=2, a=1, d=5 \\ & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 2 \\ 2 & 5\end{array}\right], A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}5 & -2 \\ -2 & 1\end{array}\right] \\ & A^{-1}=\alpha A+\beta I \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{cc}5 & -2 \\ -2 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\alpha+\beta & 2 \alpha \\ 2 \alpha & 5 \alpha+\beta\end{array}\right]} \\ & \alpha=-1, \beta=6 \rightarrow \alpha+\beta=5\end{aligned}$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> are real numbers such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f(x)=\int \frac{7 x^{10}+9 x^{8}}{\left(1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}\right)^{2}} d x, x>0, \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=0$ and $f(1)=\frac{1}{4}$. If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{1}{4} & f^{\prime}(1) & 1 \\ \alpha^{2} & 4 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})$ be such that $|\mathrm{B}|=81$, then $\alpha^{2}$ is equal to
question: Let $f(x)=\int \frac{7 x^{10}+9 x^{8}}{\left(1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}\right)^{2}} d x, x>0, \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=0$ and $f(1)=\frac{1}{4}$. If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{1}{4} & f^{\prime}(1) & 1 \\ \alpha^{2} & 4 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})$ be such that $|\mathrm{B}|=81$, then $\alpha^{2}$ is equal to
solution:
Divide numerator and denominator by $x^{18}$, let $t = x^{-9} + x^{-7} + 2$, then $dt = -(9x^{-10}+7x^{-8})dx$.
$f(x) = \frac{1}{t} + C = \frac{x^9}{1+x^2+2x^9} + C$.
$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow C = 0$. $f(1) = \frac{1}{4}$ (verified).
$f'(x) = \frac{9x^8(1+x^2+2x^9) - x^9(2x+18x^8)}{(1+x^2+2x^9)^2}$.
$f'(1) = \frac{36-20}{16} = 1$.
$|A| = \begin{vmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1/4 & 1 & 1 \\ \alpha^2 & 4 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 1 - \alpha^2$.
$|B| = |\text{adj}(\text{adj } A)| = |A|^{(3-1)^2} = (1-\alpha^2)^4 = 81 = 3^4$.
$|1-\alpha^2| = 3 \Rightarrow \alpha^2 = 4$.
